perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information means all information relating to the design, layout and operation of said Project .
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information generally refers to information broadly associated with the Project.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a clear definition of Confidential Information as 'all information relating to the design, layout and operation of said Project'. The modified text weakens this by using 'generally refers to' and 'broadly associated with', making the scope of what is considered confidential unclear.
perturbation.law_explanation: The vagueness introduced by the modified text could fail to adequately define what constitutes a 'trade secret' or confidential information under the Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act (§ 59.1-336). The original definition was much more clear, and a clear definition is required to enforce this act.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates ambiguity around the definition of 'Confidential Information'. The original text provides a clear definition while the changed text weakens this by using 'generally refers to' and 'broadly associated with'. The Law Explanation argues that this vagueness could fail to adequately define what constitutes a 'trade secret' or confidential information under the Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act (§ 59.1-336) because the original definition was much more clear. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that a trade secret is “a secret, commercially valuable plan, formula, process, or device that is used for the making, preparing, compounding, or processing of trade commodities and that can be said to be the end product of either innovation or substantial effort.” Since the definition in the modified text is now vague, it may not hold up as a 'trade secret' because its boundaries are unclear. The altered text creates an Ambiguity Legal Contradiction, because by making the term ambiguous, it's now difficult to enforce. This is direct contradiction under the definition provided.

perturbation.original_text: Person signing Agreement will sh ow title or authority to bind the firm in an agreement. Name and authorized signature must appear below. Those authorized to sign are as follows: If a sole proprietorship, the owner may sign. If a general partnership, any general partner may sign. If a limited partnership, a general partner must sign. If a limited liability company, a “member” may sign or “manager” must sign if so specified by the articles or organization. If a regular corporation, the CEO, President or Vice -President must sign.
perturbation.changed_text: The person signing this Agreement should have the authority to bind the firm. The signature of an individual believed to possess the necessary authority should appear below.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies the individuals who are authorized to sign based on the business structure. The modified text replaces this clear guidance with the ambiguous statement that the person signing 'should' have authority and be 'believed to possess the necessary authority'. This introduces uncertainty about who can legally bind the company.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification could contradict Virginia Code § 13.1-691, which addresses the authority of officers within a corporation. While it allows corporations to define officer roles, the original clause in the agreement aligned with typical corporate structures. The modified language creates uncertainty and might lead to the agreement being signed by someone without actual authority, leading to unenforceability under corporate law.

Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 13.1. Corporations Chapter 9. Virginia Stock Corporation Act 6/23/2025 § 13.1-691 . Director conflict of interests. A. A conflict of interests transaction is a transaction with the corporation in which a director of the corporation has an interest that precludes the director from being a disinterested director. A conflict of interests transaction is not voidable by the corporation solely because of the director's interest in the transaction if any one of the following is true: 1. The material facts of the transaction and the director's interest were disclosed or known to the board of directors or a committee of the board of directors and the board of directors or committee authorized, approved, or ratified the transaction; 2. The material facts of the transaction and the director's interest were disclosed to the shareholders entitled to vote and they authorized, approved, or ratified the transaction; or 3. The transaction was fair to the corporation. B. For purposes of subdivision A 1, a conflict of interests transaction is authorized, approved, or ratified if it receives the affirmative vote of a majority of the disinterested directors on the board of directors, or on the committee. A transaction shall not be authorized, approved,
perturbation.justification: The original text provided specific roles authorized to sign agreements, tailored to the business structure. The changed text replaces these specific roles with the general requirement that the signer 'should' have authority, introducing ambiguity. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation highlight that this ambiguity could lead to someone without actual authority signing, rendering the agreement unenforceable. Although the scraped snippet 1 discusses director conflicts of interest under Virginia law, it is not directly applicable here. The changed text, by creating ambiguity, undermines the assurance that the person signing has the legal authority to bind the firm, thereby creating a legal contradiction as it fails to ensure a legally authorized representative is signing as was clearly defined in the original text.
